coredata.dc:title: Synergistic effects of graphene quantum dot additives in waste plastic oil blends: Combustion stability and emission reductions analysis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Soundararajan, Gopinath
Bibin, Chidambaranathan
R, Ashok Kumar
S, Arunkumar
K, Rajesh
Devarajan, Yuvarajan
Mishra, Ruby
coredata.dc:description: This research examines the combined effects of incorporating Graphene Quantum Dot (GQD) additives into waste plastic oil blends (ULDP20) on their performance, combustion behavior, and emission characteristics in a diesel engine. The experimental study involved testing ULDP20G30, ULDP20G40, and ULDP20G50 blends, with ULDP20G40, containing 40 % GQD, identified as the most effective formulation. The ULDP20G40 blend displayed enhanced combustion stability and achieved a 5.71 % increase in Brake Thermal Efficiency (BTE) compared to ULDP20, though it remained 2.83 % lower than that of standard diesel fuel. On the emissions front, the addition of GQDs notably reduced Nitrogen Oxide (NOx) and smoke emissions when compared to ULDP20. Specifically, ULDP20G40 exhibited a 1.63 % reduction in NOx and a 2.33 % decrease in smoke emissions compared to ULDP20. However, compared to diesel, NOx and smoke emissions were slightly higher by 1.43 % and 7.69 %, respectively. The superior oxidation properties of GQDs also contributed to significant decreases in Carbon Monoxide (CO) and Unburned Hydrocarbon (UHC) emissions across all tested blends, demonstrating their effectiveness in achieving cleaner combustion processes. The experimental approach involved systematically varying the GQD concentration to identify the optimal blend, followed by an in-depth evaluation of its combustion and emission properties. These results establish GQD-enhanced ULDP20 blends, particularly ULDP20G40, as viable alternatives for sustainable fuel applications. Future work will focus on optimizing key engine parameters such as injection timing, injection pressure, and Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR) to further improve the performance and emissions profiles of GQD-enriched blends using advanced multi-objective optimization strategies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biodiesel
Renewable energy
Sustainable practices
Diesel Engine
Alternative fuels
Environmental Engineering

head.ce:title: Synergistic effects of graphene quantum dot additives in waste plastic oil blends: Combustion stability and emission reductions analysis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This research examines the combined effects of incorporating Graphene Quantum Dot (GQD) additives into waste plastic oil blends (ULDP20) on their performance, combustion behavior, and emission characteristics in a diesel engine. The experimental study involved testing ULDP20G30, ULDP20G40, and ULDP20G50 blends, with ULDP20G40, containing 40 % GQD, identified as the most effective formulation. The ULDP20G40 blend displayed enhanced combustion stability and achieved a 5.71 % increase in Brake Thermal Efficiency (BTE) compared to ULDP20, though it remained 2.83 % lower than that of standard diesel fuel. On the emissions front, the addition of GQDs notably reduced Nitrogen Oxide (NOx) and smoke emissions when compared to ULDP20. Specifically, ULDP20G40 exhibited a 1.63 % reduction in NOx and a 2.33 % decrease in smoke emissions compared to ULDP20. However, compared to diesel, NOx and smoke emissions were slightly higher by 1.43 % and 7.69 %, respectively. The superior oxidation properties of GQDs also contributed to significant decreases in Carbon Monoxide (CO) and Unburned Hydrocarbon (UHC) emissions across all tested blends, demonstrating their effectiveness in achieving cleaner combustion processes. The experimental approach involved systematically varying the GQD concentration to identify the optimal blend, followed by an in-depth evaluation of its combustion and emission properties. These results establish GQD-enhanced ULDP20 blends, particularly ULDP20G40, as viable alternatives for sustainable fuel applications. Future work will focus on optimizing key engine parameters such as injection timing, injection pressure, and Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR) to further improve the performance and emissions profiles of GQD-enriched blends using advanced multi-objective optimization strategies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The growing global emphasis on sustainable energy sources has driven extensive research into alternative fuels derived from waste materials [1]. Pyrolysis of waste plastics, particularly grocery bags, has emerged as a promising method for producing liquid hydrocarbons that can potentially replace conventional diesel fuel [2]. The liquid hydrocarbons obtained from the pyrolysis of waste polyethylene possess similar hydrocarbon structures and chain length distributions to those of petrodiesel [3]. They concluded that blends of pyrolyzed polyethylene hydrocarbons (P10–P50) could effectively fuel diesel engines, provided antioxidants are used to stabilize the fuel.

Further advancements in this domain have been achieved by blending waste plastic pyrolysis oil with various additives to enhance fuel properties and engine performance andt he effects of blending waste plastic pyrolysis oil with diethyl ether (DEE) on the direct injection engine characteristics were analysed [4]. Their findings specified that the addition of oxygenates, such as DEE, improves brake thermal efficiency by reducing viscosity and enhancing the atomization of the fuel air mixture. This modification also resulted in a reduction of pollutants like carbon monoxide, smoke, and nitrogen oxides due to improved combustion efficiency. Similarly, the use of waste plastic oil (WPO) [5] blended with Jatropha methyl ester as a diesel engine fuel were investigated. Their study revealed an increase in brake thermal efficiency (BTE) with higher engine loads, attributed to the increased heat generation within the cylinder. Additionally, they reported a decrease in emissions of carbon monoxide, smoke, and hydrocarbons, although there was a small increase in nitrogen oxide emissions.

Recent research has highlighted the potential of blends combining waste plastic oils with conventional fuels like diesel to enhance engine performance while reducing emissions. The studies demonstrated that incorporating hybrid nanoparticle additives into n-butanol, waste plastic oil, and diesel mixtures significantly improved combustion efficiency and lowered emissions, paving the way for cleaner and more effective engine operation [6]. The blends of acetone-butanol-ethanol (ABE) and waste plastic oils enhanced engine performance while reducing particulate emissions, underscoring their environmental advantages [7,8]. Similarly the use of acetone and waste plastic oil mixtures in diesel engines, reporting improvements in fuel properties and a reduction in carbon emissions [9]. These findings collectively highlight the promise of waste plastic oil-derived alternative fuels in addressing key energy and environmental challenges, offering a sustainable pathway for reducing reliance on traditional fossil fuels.

Recent studies have shown the potential benefits of incorporating nanoparticles into fuel blends. The use of Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) in diesel engines with fish oil biodiesel and ethanol blends were studied [10] and they found that GQDs, due to their unique non-zero bands, good solubility, biocompatibility, and high surface bonding characteristics, significantly enhanced combustion, increased torque by 12.42 %, and reduced emissions of carbon monoxide by 29.54 % and unburned hydrocarbons by 31.12 %. Another study on the use of GQDs in fish waste oil (FWO) [11] blends highlighted similar benefits, including a 24.59 % increase in engine power and substantial reductions in CO and UHC emissions, despite the higher NOx emissions observed with FWO blends.

The benefits of nanoparticles in fuel blends and various nanoparticles' behaviors in diesel and biodiesel blends were categorized and studied and they found that metal-based nanoparticles, such as magnesium, alumina, zinc, TiO2, and ZnO, act as oxygen enhancers during combustion, improving engine performance and reducing emissions, though maintaining the stability of these nanoparticles can be challenging [12]. The effects of carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and silver nanoparticles in diesel and biodiesel blends were examines ang reported the higher engine performance and reduced emissions with CNTs, although NOx emissions were slightly higher [13].

The performance and emission characteristics of biodiesel blends with nano additives, emphasizing that nano-additives improve heat transfer rates and thermal conductivity were reviewed and thus resulted in enhanced engine performance and reduced emissions [14]. They noted the superior dispersion stability of non-metallic CNT nanoparticles compared to metallic ones [15].

The use of nanoparticles as additives in alternative fuel blends has garnered considerable interest due to their ability to improve combustion efficiency and lower harmful emissions. Research by Yusuf et al. demonstrated that adding small amounts of CeO₂ nanoparticles to biodiesel blends enhanced combustion performance while reducing the production of pollutants such as particulate matter and gaseous emissions in diesel engines [16]. In another investigation, the combination of biodiesel and CeO₂ nanoparticles in a Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDI) diesel engine showed significant reductions in particle numbers, PM2.5 pollutants, and organic compounds, alongside improved combustion stability [17]. These studies highlight the significant role of nanoparticle additives in advancing the effectiveness and environmental sustainability of alternative fuels [18]. They also pave the way for exploring innovative additives like Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) to further optimize waste plastic oil blends.

Building on these foundational studies, the present research aims to explore the synergistic effects of incorporating Graphene Quantum Dot (GQD) additives into waste plastic oil blends. GQDs are renowned for their exceptional electrical, thermal, and mechanical properties, which are anticipated to further enhance the performance and emission characteristics of waste plastic oil blends. This comprehensive experimental study will investigate the impact of GQD additives on key fuel properties, engine performance, and emission parameters, providing new insights into the potential of advanced nanomaterials in sustainable fuel development.

The growing concerns over environmental degradation and the finite availability of fossil fuel resources have heightened the need for sustainable and efficient alternatives to conventional fuels for internal combustion engines. Among these alternatives, Waste Plastic Oil (WPO), derived from the pyrolysis of plastic waste, has gained attention for its dual benefit of reducing environmental pollution and lessening reliance on fossil fuels. Despite its potential, WPO faces significant challenges, including poor combustion stability, higher emissions, and suboptimal performance metrics, which hinder its direct application in diesel engines. Addressing these issues requires innovative approaches, including blending WPO with other fuels and incorporating advanced additives to enhance its combustion and emission characteristics.

For example, incorporating dimethyl carbonate and gasoline into diesel-corn oil blends has been shown to improve combustion efficiency and reduce harmful emissions, highlighting the effectiveness of fuel additives in enhancing fuel properties [19]. Similarly, investigations into ternary blends of diesel, corn oil, and alcohol have demonstrated improvements in energy efficiency and emission reductions, underscoring the significance of optimized fuel formulations [20].Furthermore, predictive modeling of exhaust gas temperatures for diesel-biodiesel-1-pentanol blends has provided valuable insights into optimizing engine thermal performance and emissions, showcasing the potential of tailored fuel combinations [21].

Building on these advancements, the present study evaluates the feasibility of using Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) as additives in WPO-diesel blends to enhance their performance and emissions characteristics. Due to their remarkable thermal, electrical, and catalytic properties, GQDs offer the potential to address the inherent shortcomings of WPO blends, paving the way for sustainable and efficient fuel technologies that align with global environmental and energy goals.

The novelty of this study lies in the comprehensive exploration of Graphene Quantum Dot (GQD) additives in waste plastic oil blends (ULDP20) as alternative fuels for diesel engines. The research investigates the synergistic effects of GQDs on combustion characteristics, performance metrics, and emission parameters, highlighting significant improvements in combustion efficiency and reductions in emissions such as Nitrogen Oxides (NOx), Carbon Monoxide (CO), and Unburned Hydrocarbons (UHC). Specifically, this study focuses on optimizing GQD concentration in ULDP20 blends and demonstrating how nanomaterials improve energy conversion efficiency, reduce emissions, and stabilize combustion in diesel engines. This innovative approach suggests GQDs as effective additives for enhancing the suitability and sustainability of waste plastic oil blends, contributing novel insights into advancing eco-friendly fuel technologies.

In this study, experimental trials were conducted using a ULDP20 base fuel with Graphene Quantum Dot (GQD) additives in various proportions. The preparation of these fuel blends involved several key steps to ensure the reliability and consistency of the experimental results. Three different blends of ULDP20 with GQD additives were prepared to investigate the effects of varying concentrations of GQD on engine performance and emissions. The blends were designated as follows: ULDP20G30: ULDP20 with 30 ppm of GQD, ULDP20G40: ULDP20 with 40 ppm of GQD, ULDP20G50: ULDP20 with 50 ppm of GQD. To prepare these blends, a precise amount of GQD nanoparticles was measured and thoroughly mixed with the ULDP20 base fuel. The mixing process was carried out using a magnetic stirrer to ensure uniform distribution of the GQD particles within the fuel [22]. This homogeneity is crucial for maintaining consistent combustion characteristics throughout the trials.

The physical properties of the fuel samples were critically assessed to ensure their suitability for use in diesel engines. The properties measured provide insights into the behavior and performance of the fuel blends. The following key physical properties were assessed: Viscosity is a measure of the fuel's resistance to flow and shear. It is crucial for ensuring proper atomization and combustion of the fuel within the engine. The viscosity of the fuel affects the fuel injection process, spray pattern, and ultimately the combustion efficiency [23]. Density is the mass per unit volume of the fuel and is an important parameter as it influences the fuel's energy content and combustion characteristics. Accurate density measurements are essential for calculating the fuel's energy output and ensuring consistent fuel metering in the engine [24]. The flash point is the lowermost temperature at which the fuel can turn to vapor form an flammable mixture with air. It is a critical safety parameter that indicates the fuel's volatility and flammability [25]. A higher flash point generally means the fuel is safer to handle and store. The cetane number is a measure of the fuel's ignition quality and combustion speed under compression. It indicates how readily the fuel ignites in the engine and is directly related to the engine's performance and emissions. Higher cetane numbers typically result in smoother engine operation and reduced emissions. The calorific value represents the energy content of the fuel. It is a measure of the amount of energy released during combustion. Fuels with higher calorific values can provide more energy, which can enhance engine power output and efficiency [26]. Ash content is the measure of inorganic deposit enduring after the incineration of the fuel. High ash content can lead to deposits in the engine and exhaust systems, causing wear and tear and reducing engine efficiency. It is expressed as a weight percentage (wt%). The distillation temperature at 80 % recovery provides an indication of the volatility of the fuel. It is the temperature at which 80 % of the fuel has evaporated during the distillation process. This parameter is crucial for understanding the boiling range and the evaporation characteristics of the fuel, which can affect combustion and emissions. These physical properties of the ULDP20 and GQD fuel blends were meticulously measured and compared with standard diesel fuel. The analysis provided a comprehensive understanding of how the addition of Graphene Quantum Dots (GQD) affects the fuel's characteristics and its potential effect on engine characteristics (Table 1 ).

The experimental setup and technique involved a comprehensive study to estimate the combustion, performance, and emissions characteristics of ULDP20 blends enhanced with Graphene Quantum Dots (GQD) in a direct injection (DI) diesel engine. The ULDP20 blend was prepared by mixing waste plastic oil and diesel on a volume basis. To ensure proper miscibility, the blend was observed for a week with daily checks. No separation layers were detected, confirming the homogeneity of the ULDP20 blend. The engine selected for the study was a four stroke single cylinder DI diesel engine equipped with an electrical dynamometer for load control with a power output of 4.4 kW. The injection pressure (IP) was maintained at a constant 200 bar throughout the experiments [27]. The intake air temperature was set at 27 °C. Temperature measurements were conducted using K-type thermocouples, while combustion statistics were obtained using crank angle encoders interfaced with a data acquisition system. Smoke levels were measured using an AVL smoke meter, and exhaust gas analysis (CO, NOx, HC, CO2) was performed using an AVL 444 gas analyzer. Initially, the diesel engine was run using conventional diesel fuel for approximately one hour to reach steady-state conditions. This step was critical to stabilize the engine's operating parameters and ensure that subsequent measurements were not influenced by transient conditions or residues from previous fuels. Achieving steady-state conditions helped in establishing a baseline for comparing the performance and emissions of the various ULDP20 and GQD blends.

Prior to testing the experimental blends, the engine was preheated by operating it without a load for 30 min using regular diesel fuel. The standard injection timing (SIT) was set to 23° bTDC, and the standard injection pressure (SIP) was maintained at 200 bar, with an admitting air temperature of 27 °C [28]. After warm-up, the engine load was gradually increased in steps of 25 % using the electrical dynamometer until reaching full load (100 %). At each load condition, the engine was allowed to stabilize for 30 min to ensure consistent combustion performance [29]. Data acquisition was performed during these stabilized periods to capture reliable performance and emission metrics. The experimental phase involved testing three different blends of ULDP20 enhanced with GQD additives: ULDP20G30: ULDP20 with 30 ppm of GQD, ULDP20G40: ULDP20 with 40 ppm of GQD, ULDP20G50: ULDP20 with 50 ppm of GQD. Each blend was sequentially tested under various loading conditions at a fixed engine speed of 1500 rpm. The testing procedure included fueling the engine with each ULDP20GQD blend, running a 30-minute warm-up period at no load, incrementally increasing the load in 25 % steps, and allowing the engine to stabilize for 30 min at each load point for data collection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Uncertainty analysis in engine performance and emission measurements
ce:section.ce:para:
Accurate estimation of engine performance and emission characteristics is essential in experimental investigations. However, measurements can be affected by various errors, necessitating an uncertainty analysis to determine the accuracy of the results. According to [30], standard operating procedures are provided to identify and quantify errors during experimental investigations. Errors can arise from several sources, including human error, ecological conditions, standardization error, and instrumental error. The uncertainty limits for the experimental measurements are estimated using the root sum square (RSS) technique. The overall uncertainty (UE) is calculated using the following formula
U E
=
∑
j = 1
n
Z
j
2
For this experiment, the uncertainties of BP, TFC, EGT, NOx, CO, UHC and smoke are symbolized as Z1, Z2, Z3, Z4, Z5, Z6, and Z7, respectively.
U E
=
Z 1 2
+
Z 2 2
+
Z 3 2
+
Z 4 2
+
Z 5 2
+
Z 6 2
+
Z 7 2
U E
=
1.2
2
+
1
2
+
0.1
2
+
0.2
2
+
0.2
2
+
0.2
2
+
1
2
U E
=
1.44 + 1.00 + 0.01 + 0.04 + 0.04 + 0.04 + 1.00
U E
= ± 1.89 %

Thus, the overall uncertainty in the experimental measurements is ±1.89 %. This analysis accounts for potential errors from environmental conditions, human error, calibration error, and instrumental error, ensuring that the results are interpreted with the appropriate level of accuracy.

The study focused on observing key combustion characteristics—cylinder pressure, Combustion Heat Release Rate (CHRR), Heat Release Rate (HRR), Ignition Delay (ID), and Burn Duration (BD)—for ULDP20GQD blends, comparing them with ULDP20 and diesel. These parameters were meticulously analyzed to understand how the addition of Graphene Quantum Dots (GQD) influences combustion behavior in the engine. The experimental setup ensured consistent conditions across tests, with the engine running at 1500 rpm under varying load levels. Data acquisition systems recorded cylinder pressure profiles, facilitating the calculation of CHRR, HRR, ID, and CD for each blend. Results were then compared against baseline data from ULDP20 and standard diesel fuel, highlighting any improvements or deviations attributed to GQD additives. This comprehensive approach aimed to provide insights into the combustion dynamics and efficiency gains achievable with ULDP20GQD blends, crucial for assessing their viability as sustainable diesel fuel alternatives.

Fig. 2 depicts the deviation of HRR with crank angle for ULDP20, ULDP20GQD additive blends, and diesel. HRR is crucial as it influences the formation of NOx through mechanisms like the Zeldovich process during combustion. Under maximum load conditions (100 %), the maximum HRR (J/ °CA) were 47.82 for diesel, 49.86 for ULDP20, 47.01 for ULDP20G30, 44.72 for ULDP20G40, and 41.26 for ULDP20G50. Significantly, ULDP20G40 exhibited a 6.49 % lower maximum HRR compared to raw diesel, attributable to a shorter Burn Duration (BD) and enhanced fuel oxidation rates facilitated by Graphene Quantum Dots (GQD) additives. The shorter BD indicates that the combustion phase is more concentrated within the power stroke, reducing the duration of the energy release process. This focused release, coupled with GQD's ability to distribute thermal energy evenly and enhance the surface reactivity of the fuel molecules, ensures that the majority of the combustion occurs at a controlled and moderate rate, rather than spiking to higher HRR values typically observed in conventional diesel combustion. This reduction in HRR also contributes to lower NOx emissions [31]. Moreover, ULDP20G40 showed a 10.30 % decrease in maximum HRR compared to ULDP20 at full load, indicating improved combustion efficiency and effectiveness of GQD additives in optimizing the combustion process [7]. These results highlight the potential of ULDP20G40 and similar blends to achieve lower HRR and abridged emissions compared to traditional diesel and ULDP20.

Fig. 3 illustrates the deviation of cylinder pressure with crank angle for ULDP20, ULDP20GQD additive blends, and diesel. Cylinder peak pressure is a critical parameter in combustion analysis, indicating the efficiency of fuel combustion during the rapid combustion phase. Under conditions of maximum load (100 %), the peak pressures in the cylinders were recorded as follows: 58.97 bar for diesel, 59.31 bar for ULDP20, 59.01 bar for ULDP20G30, 57.36 bar for ULDP20G40, and 54.84 bar for ULDP20G50. Notably, ULDP20G40 exhibited a cylinder peak pressure 2.73 % lower than diesel, suggesting enhanced combustion efficiency due to the higher intrinsic mobility of GQD additives [32]. The catalytic properties of GQDs improve the oxidation kinetics of the fuel, enabling faster chemical reactions at lower ignition delays. This results in earlier and more efficient combustion within the power stroke, which can contribute to slightly lower peak pressures while still maintaining high combustion efficiency. The suppression of excessive pressure buildup also reduces the thermal and mechanical stress on engine components, contributing to a more stable and durable engine operation.

The presence of Graphene Quantum Dots in ULDP20 blends facilitated smoother combustion, leading to a decrease in cylinder peak pressure as the concentration of additives increased. Specifically, ULDP20G40 showed a 3.29 % reduction in cylinder peak pressure compared to ULDP20. This reduction is attributed to the lower heat release rate, resulting in a shorter ignition delay [33]. The addition of Graphene additives promoted faster fuel oxidation, thereby optimizing the combustion process in the engine. These findings underscore the potential of GQD additives to improve combustion efficiency in ULDP20 blends, offering insights into their role in enhancing the performance and sustainability of diesel engines.

Fig. 3 illustrates the deviation of CHRR versus crank angle for ULDP20, ULDP20GQD additive blends, and diesel. CHRR represents the total heat energy out per degree of crank angle and provides crucial insights into the progression of combustion within the engine. Under maximum load conditions (100 %), the CHRR were recorded as 1359.5, 1344.5, 1311.68, 1277.088 and 1213.23 J/ °CA for diesel, ULDP20, ULDP20G30, ULDP20G40 and ULDP20G50 respectively. Notably, ULDP20G40 exhibited a 6.01 % lower CHRR compared to diesel, attributed to its lower cylinder pressure. This reduction in CHRR also correlates with lower NOx emissions. The slightly lower cylinder pressure observed in ULDP20G40 indicates a smoother and more controlled combustion process. This smoother combustion results in a less abrupt heat release, which translates to a lower CHRR. GQDs, due to their catalytic properties and excellent thermal conductivity, enhance the combustion process by improving fuel oxidation and enabling a more uniform energy release throughout the combustion phase. This distributed heat release contributes to lower peak CHRR values. Moreover, ULDP20G40 showed a 4.96 % decrease in CHRR compared to ULDP20 at full load. This reduction indicates enhanced combustion competence facilitated by the presence of Graphene Quantum Dots (GQD) additives, which enhance fuel oxidation rates and contribute to a more efficient combustion process [34]. These findings underscore the potential of ULDP20GQD blends, particularly ULDP20G40, to achieve lower CHRR and emissions compared to diesel and ULDP20. This suggests a promising avenue for enhancing the performance and environmental sustainability of diesel engines through the use of GQD additives.

Fig. 4 illustrates the deviation of Ignition Delay (ID) with brake power for ULDP20, ULDP20GQD additive blends, and diesel. Ignition delay represents the period between the start of fuel inoculation and the commencement of combustion, measured in degrees of crank angle ( °CA), reflecting both physical and chemical delays during the combustion progression [26]. For diesel, the ignition delay ( °CA) ranges from 7.1 at 25 % load to 7.2 at 100 % load. Similarly, for ULDP20, it varies from 7.2 at 25 % load to 7.29 at 100 % load. At full load, ULDP20G30, ULDP20G40, and ULDP20G50 exhibit ignition delays of 7.17, 7.04, and 6.94 respectively. The presence of Graphene Quantum Dots (GQD) additives introduces additional voids that enhance the attraction and mobility of reactants during combustion, resulting in shorter ignition delays. Specifically, ULDP20G40 demonstrates ignition delays of 6.98 °CA, 6.49 °CA, 6.60 °CA, and 7.04 °CA at 25, 50, 75, and 100 percentage loads respectively. This reduction in ignition delay indicates improved combustion efficiency facilitated by GQD additives. The role of GQD additives in optimizing combustion processes, leading to shorter ignition delays and potentially improving engine performance across various load conditions compared to conventional ULDP20 and diesel fuels.

Fig. 5 illustrates the deviation of Burn Duration (BD) with brake power for ULDP20, ULDP20GQD additive mixtures, and diesel. Burn Duration refers to the period during which 10 to 90 percentage of burning occurs in the rapid combustion stage, measured in degrees of crank angle ( °CA). For diesel, the Burn Duration ( °CA) ranges from 32.1 at 25 % load to 36.4 at 100 % load. Similarly, for ULDP20, it varies from 31.0 at 25 % load to 35.4 at 100 % load. At full load, ULDP20G30, ULDP20G40, and ULDP20G50 exhibit Burn Durations of 36.1, 36.8, and 37.13 respectively. ULDP20G40 shows a longer Burn Duration as compared to other blends due to its lower Heat Release Rate (HRR). The presence of Graphene Quantum Dots (GQD) additives, known for their high surface area, enhances heat transfer rates during combustion [35]. GQDs enhance the uniformity of the combustion process by acting as nano-catalysts. Their ability to facilitate faster oxidation reactions helps sustain combustion over a longer duration while avoiding abrupt peaks in HRR. Specifically, the Burn Duration for ULDP20G40 at 25 %, 50 %, 75 %, and 100 % loads was observed at 32.4 °CA, 33.6 °CA, 35.9 °CA, and 36.8 °CA respectively. These results underscore the impact of GQD additives on combustion dynamics, influencing Burn Duration and heat transfer efficiency across varying load conditions.

Fig. 6 illustrates the deviation of Cylinder Peak Pressure with brake power for ULDP20, ULDP20GQD additive blends, and diesel. Cylinder peak pressure is a critical indicator of combustion efficiency and engine performance, measured in bar. For diesel, cylinder peak pressure (bar) ranges from 52.3 at 25 % load to 58.97 at 100 % load. Similarly, for ULDP20, it varies from 52.68 at 25 % load to 59.31 at 100 % load. At full load, ULDP20G30, ULDP20G40, and ULDP20G50 exhibit cylinder peak pressures of 59.01, 57.36, and 54.84 respectively. Notably, ULDP20G40 shows a 2.73 % lower cylinder peak pressure compared to raw diesel, attributed to a longer Burn Duration (BD) and shorter Ignition Delay (ID) [36]. GQDs possess a high density of reactive functional groups on their surfaces. These groups act as active sites, accelerating oxidation reactions during combustion by lowering the activation energy required for hydrocarbon breakdown. Moreover, ULDP20G40 demonstrates a 3.29 % decrease in cylinder peak pressure compared to ULDP20 at full load. Specifically, cylinder peak pressures for ULDP20G40 at 25, 50, 75, and 100 percent loads were observed at 50.24 bar, 52.44 bar, 55.56 bar, and 57.36 bar respectively. GQDs contribute to improved atomization and better mixing of the fuel-air mixture. This uniform distribution ensures that combustion occurs evenly across the cylinder, avoiding localized hotspots that could cause abrupt spikes in the Heat Release Rate (HRR). These findings underscore the impact of Graphene Quantum Dots (GQD) additives on optimizing combustion parameters, leading to improved engine efficiency and reduced peak pressures compared to conventional ULDP20 and diesel fuels.

Fig. 7 illustrates the deviation of Brake Thermal Efficiency versus brake power for ULDP20, ULDP20GQD additive blends, and diesel. BTE represents the efficiency with which fuel energy is transformed into useful work by the engine, expressed as a percentage. Under maximum load conditions (100 %), BTEs were measured at 29.80 % for diesel, 27.39 % for ULDP20, 28.38 % for ULDP20G30, 28.96 % for ULDP20G40, and 28.09 % for ULDP20G50. Notably, ULDP20G40 exhibits a BTE 2.83 % lower than diesel, attributed to factors such as lower fuel consumption and reduced fuel burnout time facilitated by Graphene Quantum Dots (GQD) additives. The altered physical properties of ULDP20G40 (compared to diesel) can impact atomization and spray characteristics, slightly reducing the efficiency of energy conversion. However, ULDP20G40 shows a 5.71 % higher BTE compared to ULDP20 at full load, underscoring the efficiency gains achieved with GQD additives. The increased thermal conductivity of GQD additives improves combustion rates, contributing to improved BTE across various load conditions [37]. Specifically, BTEs for ULDP20G40 at 25 %, 50 %, 75 %, and 100 % loads were observed at 17.76 %, 25.81 %, 29.10 %, and 28.96 % respectively. Faster combustion can lead to higher localized temperatures during certain phases, causing a portion of the heat to be lost to the cylinder walls rather than contributing to useful work. These findings highlight the potential of GQD additives to enhance engine efficiency and performance metrics like BTE, suggesting their role in advancing the operation of alternative fuels such as ULDP20 in diesel engines.

Fig. 8 depicts the deviation of Brake Specific Fuel Consumption versus brake power for ULDP20, ULDP20GQD additive blends, and diesel. BSFC measures the amount of fuel disbursed per unit of useful work produced by the engine, expressed in kg/kW-hr. At 100 % load, BSFC (kg/kW-hr) values were recorded as 0.284 for diesel, 0.309 for ULDP20, 0.294 for ULDP20G30, 0.288 for ULDP20G40, and 0.299 for ULDP20G50. ULDP20G40 shows a 1.34 % higher BSFC compared to diesel, attributed to its shorter ID. The high surface area and thermal conductivity of GQDs improve the homogeneity of combustion, ensuring efficient energy release during the power stroke, thereby reducing the amount of fuel required to produce the same power output. However, GQD additive blends exhibit lower BSFC due to faster combustion and reduced heat release rates compared to conventional ULDP20 blends [38]. Notably, ULDP20G40 demonstrates a 6.85 % lower BSFC compared to ULDP20. Specifically, BSFC (kg/kW-hr), values for ULDP20G40 at 25, 50, 75, and 100 Percent loads were observed at 0.469, 0.323,0.286, and 0.288 respectively. These results highlight the potential of GQD additives to improve fuel efficiency by reducing BSFC [39], thereby enhancing the overall performance and sustainability of diesel engines when using alternative fuels like ULDP20.

Fig. 9 exemplifies the deviation of EGT vs BP for ULDP20, ULDP20GQD additive blends, and diesel. EGT represents the temperature of the exhaust gases exiting the engine, measured in degrees Celsius. The exhaust gas temperatures ( °C) were recorded as 455 for diesel, 482 for ULDP20, 465.8 for ULDP20G30, 464.1 for ULDP20G40, and 467.5 for ULDP20G50. ULDP20G40 exhibits a 2 % higher EGT when compared to raw diesel, primarily due to its longer Burn Duration. This extended burn duration allows for more complete combustion, resulting in a higher heat release during the combustion cycle. The enhanced combustion is attributed to the properties of the Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) additives, which improve the oxidative reactivity of the fuel blend. These additives facilitate better mixing of air and fuel, leading to sustained combustion and higher thermal energy output, which manifests as an increased EGT. Though, at full load, ULDP20G40 shows a 3.71 % lower EGT compared to ULDP20, attributed to the increased surface area of Graphene Quantum Dots (GQD) additives, which promote a more efficient combustion process and propagate the flame effectively throughout combustion [38]. This increased surface area promotes more efficient oxidation of the fuel, ensuring a more uniform and effective flame propagation throughout the combustion chamber. The result is a reduction in localized hotspots and heat losses, leading to a slightly lower EGT despite the improved combustion efficiency. Specifically, EGT values for ULDP20G40 at 25 %, 50 %, 75 %, and 100 % loads were observed at 210.1 °C, 281.5 °C, 373.3 °C, and 464.1 °C respectively. These results underscore the impact of GQD additives on reducing exhaust gas temperatures and improving combustion efficiency, indicating their potential in enhancing the performance and environmental sustainability of diesel engines using alternative fuels like ULDP20.

Fig. 10 presents the deviation of NOx with BP for ULDP20, ULDP20GQD additive blends, and diesel. NOx emissions denote the quantity of nitrogen oxides released per unit of energy produced, measured in grams per kilowatt-hour (g/kWh). At maximum load (100 %), NOx emissions (g/kWh) were measured at 11.88 for diesel, 12.25 for ULDP20, 12.19 for ULDP20G30, 12.05 for ULDP20G40, and 12.12 for ULDP20G50. ULDP20G40 shows a 1.43 % higher NOx emission when compared with standard diesel, primarily owing to its lower heat release rate. However, at full load, ULDP20G40 exhibits a 1.63 % lower NOx emission compared to ULDP20, indicating improved combustion efficiency facilitated by Graphene Quantum Dots (GQD) additives. The high thermal conductivity of GQD additives improves the evaporation amount of fuel droplets, contributing to a significant reduction in NOx emissions [12]. The enhanced thermal distribution facilitated by GQDs prevents localized overheating, maintaining a more uniform temperature profile within the chamber. This reduced occurrence of high-temperature zones directly leads to lower thermal NOx formation, while still achieving efficient combustion. The rapid flame propagation and stable combustion prevent incomplete combustion byproducts, ensuring the energy is utilized efficiently without the excessive heat spikes that favor NOx formation. Specifically, NOx emissions for ULDP20G40 at 25 %, 50 %, 75 %, and 100 % loads were observed at 15.44, 12.64, 12.28, and 12.05 respectively. These findings highlight the potential of GQD additives in mitigating NOx emissions and improving combustion efficiency in diesel engines using alternative fuels like ULDP20, thereby supporting environmental sustainability efforts in the transportation sector.

Fig. 11 illustrates the deviation of UHC Vs BP for ULDP20, ULDP20GQD additive blends, and diesel. UHC emissions represent the amount of hydrocarbon compounds that remain unburned in the exhaust gases, measured in grams per kilowatt-hour (g/kWh) [40]. At maximum load (100 %), UHC (g/kWh) emissions were recorded at 0.160 for diesel, 0.161 for ULDP20, 0.1609 for ULDP20G30, 0.156 for ULDP20G40, and 0.1607 for ULDP20G50. ULDP20G40 shows a 2.75 % lower UHC emission compared to diesel, attributed to increased surface area and higher catalytic activity facilitated by Graphene Quantum Dots (GQD) additives, which promote cleaner combustion [41]. The catalytic effect ensures that even hydrocarbons that might otherwise remain unburned in conventional diesel combustion are oxidized, resulting in lower UHC emissions. Furthermore, GQD additive blends exhibit consistently lower UHC emissions across all loads compared to both diesel and ULDP20 blends. Specifically, at full load, ULDP20G40 demonstrates a 3.71 % lower UHC emission compared to ULDP20. The observed UHC emissions for ULDP20 at 25, 50, 75, and 100 percentage loads were 0.332, 0.234, 0.186, and 0.156 respectively. These results underscore the effectiveness of GQD additives in reducing hydrocarbon pollutants emitted from diesel engines, highlighting their role in achieving cleaner and more efficient combustion with alternative fuels like ULDP20.

Fig. 12 portrays the deviation of CO Vs BP for ULDP20, ULDP20GQD additive blends, and diesel. The emission of CO represents the amount of carbon monoxide released per unit of energy produced, measured in grams per kilowatt-hour (g/kWh). At maximum load (100 %), CO emissions were measured at 5.340 for diesel, 5.607 for ULDP20, 5.401 for ULDP20G30, 5.212 for ULDP20G40, and 5.500 for ULDP20G50. ULDP20G40 shows a 2.40 % lesser CO emission compared to diesel, attributed to the presence of oxygenated additives that facilitate complete combustion and improve oxidation rates [14]. Graphene Quantum Dots contain functional groups with oxygen-bearing species that play a crucial role in improving the oxidation process during combustion. The additional oxygen from these additives helps in converting CO, an intermediate combustion byproduct, into Carbon Dioxide (CO₂), thereby reducing the concentration of CO emissions in the exhaust. Moreover, at full load, ULDP20G40 exhibits a 7.04 % lower CO emission compared to ULDP20, indicating enhanced combustion efficiency with reduced CO emissions in GQD additive blends. Reduced CO emissions were consistently observed across all GQD additive blends compared to ULDP20. Specifically, CO emissions for ULDP20 at 25 %, 50 %, 75 %, and 100 % loads were detected at 14.224, 7.259, 5.941, and 5.212 respectively. These findings highlight the beneficial effects of GQD additives in reducing carbon monoxide emissions from diesel engines, contributing to improved environmental performance and combustion efficiency when using alternative fuels like ULDP20.

Fig. 13 illustrates the deviation of CO2 emissions with BP for ULDP20, ULDP20GQD additive blends, and diesel. CO2 emissions epitomise the amount of carbon dioxide released per unit of energy produced, measured in grams per kilowatt-hour (g/kWh). At maximum load condition (100 %), CO2 emissions were recorded at 705 for diesel, 653.2 for ULDP20, 680 for ULDP20G30, 722.25 for ULDP20G40, and 666.26 for ULDP20G50. ULDP20G40 shows a 2.45 % higher CO2 emission compared to diesel, attributed to its improved oxidation rate facilitated by Graphene Quantum Dots (GQD) additives [42]. However, at full load, ULDP20G40 exhibits a 10.57 % higher CO2 emission compared to ULDP20, indicating more complete fuel burning due to enhanced combustion efficiency facilitated by GQD additives. Higher CO₂ emissions indicate that more of the carbon in the fuel has been completely oxidized, as opposed to forming incomplete combustion byproducts such as Carbon Monoxide (CO) or Unburned Hydrocarbons (UHC). The oxygen-bearing functional groups on GQDs provide additional oxygen during the combustion process, promoting the full oxidation of carbon-based fuel molecules. GQD additives have the capability to enhance complete oxidation by attaching to functional group edges, thereby promoting more thorough combustion [12]. Specifically, CO2 emissions for ULDP20G40 at 25 %, 50 %, 75 %, and 100 % loads were observed at 1329.65, 893.2, 738.92, and 722.25 respectively, demonstrating the varying emissions levels across different operating conditions. These results underscore the complex interactions between fuel composition, combustion efficiency, and emissions characteristics when utilizing GQD additives in alternative fuels like ULDP20, contributing to both challenges and opportunities in optimizing environmental and performance outcomes in diesel engines.

Fig. 14 illustrates the deviation of Smoke emissions Vs BP for ULDP20, ULDP20GQD additive blends, and diesel. Smoke emissions are quantified in terms of BSU (Black Smoke Units), which measure the opacity of smoke released from the engine. At maximum load condition (100 %), smoke emissions were recorded at 3.90 for diesel, 4.30 for ULDP20, 4.34 for ULDP20G30, 4.20 for ULDP20G40, and 4.57 for ULDP20G50. ULDP20G40 exhibits a smoke emission 7.69 % increased than diesel, attributed to the heavier carbon content present in GQD additives. However, at maximum load, ULDP20G40 shows a 2.33 % lower smoke emission compared to ULDP20. This reduction is due to the shorter ID and lower Heat Release Rate (HRR) facilitated by GQD additives, which promote more efficient combustion and reduce the formation of smoke particles [43]. Smoke is formed when hydrocarbons undergo pyrolysis instead of complete oxidation, resulting in soot precursors like acetylene and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs). The catalytic properties of GQDs facilitate the oxidation of these precursors before they aggregate into soot particles. The oxygenated functional groups on GQDs also provide additional oxygen, ensuring a more complete combustion process that limits soot formation. Specifically, smoke emissions for ULDP20G40 at 25 %, 50 %, 75 %, and 100 % loads were observed at 0.55, 1.84, 3.79, and 4.20 respectively, indicating varying levels of smoke opacity under different operating conditions. These findings highlight the role of GQD additives in influencing smoke emissions from diesel engines, emphasizing their potential in mitigating environmental impacts by improving combustion efficiency and reducing particulate emissions.

This research highlights the potential of Graphene Quantum Dot (GQD) additives in improving the performance and emission characteristics of waste plastic oil blends (ULDP20) used in diesel engines. Among the various blends evaluated, ULDP20G40, with a 40 % concentration of GQDs, demonstrated the most notable advancements in combustion stability and emission control when compared to both ULDP20 and traditional diesel fuel.
•
The Brake Thermal Efficiency (BTE) of ULDP20G40 was observed to be 2.83 % lower than that of diesel. However, it showed a 5.71 % enhancement compared to ULDP20, indicating improved energy conversion efficiency.
•
The incorporation of GQDs significantly reduced smoke and Nitrogen Oxide (NOx) emissions. ULDP20G40 achieved a 1.63 % reduction in smoke relative to ULDP20, although NOx emissions were slightly higher than those of conventional diesel.
•
GQDs contributed to improved oxidation processes, leading to notable reductions in Carbon Monoxide (CO) and Unburned Hydrocarbon (UHC) emissions across all tested blends.

These findings emphasize the promise of GQD-infused waste plastic oil blends, particularly ULDP20G40, as sustainable alternatives to conventional fuels. Further research should aim to optimize engine operating parameters, such as Injection Timing, Injection Pressure, and the use of Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR), to enhance overall performance and minimize emissions, thereby advancing cleaner and more efficient fuel solutions.

Gopinath Soundararajan: Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Chidambaranathan Bibin: Methodology, Funding acquisition. Ashok Kumar R: Resources, Project administration. Arunkumar S: Software, Project administration. Rajesh K: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Yuvarajan Devarajan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Ruby Mishra: Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.rineng.2025.104130.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Characterisation and effect of using waste plastic oil and diesel fuel blends in compression ignition engine
Energy
